text.title: gateway-fallback-chain Core Rules
text.summary: Five testable rules for production gateway-based model routing with fallback.

rule.statement: Configure every production LLM call with a primary model AND at least one fallback model from a different provider; same-vendor fallbacks do not protect against vendor-wide incidents.
rule.rationale: Anthropic, OpenAI, and Google have all had multi-hour outages affecting all their models simultaneously. Cross-provider fallbacks are the only path to availability above the single-vendor floor.

rule.statement: Surface 4xx client errors (400, 401, 422) WITHOUT fallback; these are agent bugs, not provider incidents, and silent retry masks them.
rule.rationale: Falling back on a 400 ships a broken request to every provider, multiplying the failure surface and hiding the bug.

rule.statement: If the workload is subject to data-residency or compliance constraints, EITHER self-host the gateway inside the same boundary OR call the provider SDK directly and implement the fallback chain in application code.
rule.rationale: Hosted gateways route data through third-party infrastructure; that breaks residency contracts and adds processors to the audit scope.

rule.statement: For workloads that depend on vendor-specific features (Anthropic prompt caching, OpenAI Batch API, fine-tunes), call the vendor SDK directly and implement fallback in app code; do NOT route through a gateway.
rule.rationale: The 90% cache discount on cached input is far larger than gateway routing savings; gateways typically lose the cache.

rule.statement: Emit OTel GenAI spans for every gateway call with attributes `primary_model`, `used_model`, `fallback_count`, and `cost_usd`; alert when `fallback_count` rises above a baseline.
rule.rationale: Rising fallback rate is the leading indicator of vendor incidents and quota exhaustion; without per-call telemetry the first signal is angry users.
